header: Gerente de People & Culture — Maple Bear
hub_subtitle: Business English para People & Culture, comunicação executiva e a liderança da divisão do Canadá

lesson.menu_title: The Unwritten Rules -- Rapport and Etiquette in the Canadian Office
lesson.menu_desc: Your first weeks physically in the Canadian office, decoding the etiquette nobody hands you in a document: the small talk that has to come before business, the flat, egalitarian way people speak to their VP, the punctuality that is not a suggestion, the understatement that hides a real objection. And the language to voice your own adaptation without sounding like you are complaining -- rapport, small talk, egalitarian, cordiality, punctuality, formality, understatement, code-switching, etiquette, reciprocity, to break the ice, to read the room, to hit it off, to save face, the unwritten rules. Structure: wish / if only for cultural adaptation (I wish the team made more small talk / I wish I had asked about the etiquette sooner / I wish people wouldn't read my silence as agreement / if only I had read the room). -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 13 -- The Unwritten Rules
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Danielle Moreira | Lesson 13 -- The Unwritten Rules

listenings.text: Danielle, it is Nathan, from People and Culture here in Toronto. I am recording this before your first full week, because there is a whole layer of this office that will not be in any onboarding document, and I wish somebody had recorded something like this for me when I moved the other way, from Vancouver into a team that ran on rules nobody had ever written down. So here are three of them. First, the small talk is not filler. In Brazil you told me people warm up fast and get personal quickly; here it is the opposite on the surface. People will spend five minutes on the weekend and the weather before anyone says a real thing, and if you skip that to be efficient, you do not read as focused, you read as cold. The small talk is how we break the ice, and it is where the rapport is actually built. Second, we are flatter than you expect. Our VP is called by her first name, she lines up for coffee like everyone else, and if you defer to her too visibly in a meeting, people find it a little uncomfortable, not respectful. It is an egalitarian style, and it takes some getting used to. And third, watch the understatement. When a Canadian says something is not quite there yet, or that it is interesting, that is often a real objection wearing a polite coat. Learn to read the room, because the disagreement is in the room, it is just not on the table. None of this means you change who you are. It means you code-switch, the same way you already do between Portuguese and English. Give it a month. You will hit it off with this team faster than you think.

Danielle, it is Ruth, your coach. You sent me a long message that was basically a list of things that frustrate you about the Canadian office, and I want to do something useful with it, because the frustration is not the problem. The problem is that it has nowhere to go. So here is the tool for this week. Every one of those frustrations, say it to yourself starting with I wish. Not to complain, but to name precisely what you would change. And notice what the grammar forces you to do. When you say I wish the team made more small talk, you are not describing reality, so English pulls the verb back into the past, made, even though you mean now. That little backward step is the language admitting this is not how things are, this is how I would want them. Then there is the sharper one, the regret. When you say I wish I had asked about the etiquette on day one, the had asked is you looking back and wanting a different past, and that is the most senior thing you can do with a mistake, name it without drowning in it. And when it is other people you want to change, I wish they would stop reading my silence as agreement, the would carries the wanting. Here is why this matters more than it looks. A leader who only says this is wrong sounds like she is resisting the culture. A leader who says I wish it worked differently, and here is what I am doing meanwhile, sounds like she is adapting to it. Same frustration. Completely different person in the room. If only you had had this framing in your first week, you said. Well, you have it now, and the first week is not the week that counts.

items.situation: "You joined a meeting and went straight to the agenda, and it landed badly. Say what you wish you had done, and what you will do next time."
tips: I wish I had spent the first few minutes on small talk instead of opening with the agenda. In Brazil that would have read as respect for their time; here it read as cold. Next time I will break the ice first, because that is where the rapport starts.
wish + had + past participle for the regret, then a plain future for the fix -- name it, do not dwell on it

items.situation: "A colleague keeps calling the VP by her first name and you keep saying 'Ms. Carter'. You feel out of step. Voice it."
tips: I wish someone had told me on day one how egalitarian the address is here. I kept using the formality I would use in Brazil, and it created a small distance I did not intend. I am code-switching now -- first names, same respect.
wish + past perfect for what you did not know; contrast Brazilian formality with the Canadian egalitarian norm

items.situation: "In a review, the team said your plan was 'interesting' and 'not quite there yet', and you took it as approval. Reflect on it."
tips: I wish I had read the room instead of the words. 'Interesting' was an understatement -- it was a polite objection, and I missed it because I was listening for a Brazilian-style direct no. If only I had asked one follow-up question, I would have caught it.
wish + past perfect + if only for the missed signal; name the understatement as the thing you now watch for

items.situation: "You want the team to stop treating your quiet, thinking pauses as agreement. Say it as a wish, not a complaint."
tips: I wish people wouldn't read my silence as consent. When I go quiet I am usually weighing something, not agreeing, and I would rather they asked. I am learning to say 'let me think on that out loud' so I do not save face at the cost of clarity.
wish + would for a behavior in others you want changed -- it carries the wanting without sounding like an accusation

items.situation: "A peer asks how you are settling in. Be honest about the adjustment without sounding like you regret the move."
tips: Honestly, I wish I were further along with the unwritten rules, but I am hitting it off with the team and the etiquette is starting to make sense. The reciprocity here is real -- people invest in you once you invest in the small talk first.
wish + were (formal subjunctive) for the present adjustment; keep it warm -- an adaptation story, not a regret

items.situation: "Summarize, for your mentor back in Brazil, the one thing you wish you had known before you arrived."
tips: If only I had understood that the culture is carried in the small things -- the small talk, the first names, the understatement -- and not in the org chart. I read the policies before I came; I wish I had asked about the unwritten rules, because that is where the real work of belonging happens.
if only + wish + past perfect for the headline lesson; close on the unwritten rules as the real curriculum
